docs.text: Big idea: Photon beams deposit dose indirectly. The photon transfers energy to charged particles, and those charged particles deposit energy in tissue (i.e. Dose).

docs.title: What is  Radiation?
docs.text:
• "Radiation" is the transmission of energy via waves or particles.
  • Radiation can be:
    • Electromagnetic waves or particles with mass.
    • Charged or uncharged
    • Ionizing or non-ionizing ()
  • Planck-Einstein relationship describes the relationship between energy (\(E\)), frequency (\(v\)), and wavelength (\(\lambda\)):
\[ E = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda} \]

where:

Symbol Definition \(E\) Photon energy \(h\) Planck's constant (\(6.626 \times 10^{-34}\) J·s) \(\nu\) Frequency of the photon \(c\) Speed of light (\(3.00 \times 10^8\) m/s) \(\lambda\) Wavelength of the photon

docs.title: What is Ionizing Radiation?
docs.text:
• Radiation energy high enough to detach electron from atom \(\rightarrow\) able to cause DNA damage.
  • Threshold is 12 eV for ionization in water and 34 eV for creation of an ion pair in air.

Exam Tips

  • Water ionization energy to free an electron ≈ 12.6 eV
  • Mean energy per ion pair in air ≈ 34 eV
  • For a 6 MV photon: \(\lambda \approx 2.1 \times 10^{-13}\,\mathrm{m} \approx 0.2\,\mathrm{pm}\)

docs.title: Fundamental Particle Types
docs.text: Particle Symbol Charge Rest Mass Photon \(\gamma\) 0 0 Electron \(e^-\) -1 0.511 MeV/\(c^2\) Positron \(e^+\) +1 0.511 MeV/\(c^2\) Proton \(p\) +1 938.3 MeV/\(c^2\) Neutron \(n\) 0 939.6 MeV/\(c^2\) Alpha Particle \(\alpha\) +2 3727 MeV/\(c^2\) ___

docs.title: Bremsstrahlung Radiation: "Braking" Radiation
docs.text:
• Mechanism of Bremsstrahlung X-ray Production:
    • High energy \(e^-\) \(\rightarrow\) Redirected \(e^-\) + γ from \(\Delta\) kinetic energy.
    • Incident \(e-\) is deflected by the nuclear Coulomb field \(\rightarrow\) Change of electron direction gives up kinetic energy, which is emitted as an γ photon.
  • Key Points:
    • Continuous energy spectrum.
    • Dominant X-ray production modality in Linacs.
      • Bremsstrahlung productions is proportional to:
        • Electron energy (\(E\))
        • Atomic number (Z)
\[ Bremsstrahlung \propto ZE \]

!!! tip "Exam Tip" Bremsstrahlung production increases with: - Increasing atomic number (Z) - Increasing electron energy (E) Therefore tungsten (Z = 74) is an excellent linac target material.

docs.title: Characteristic Radiation
docs.text:
• Mechanism of Characteristic X-ray Production:

    • An incident electron ejects an inner-shell electron. \(\rightarrow\) An outer-shell electron fills the vacancy. \(\rightarrow\) The energy difference is emitted as an x-ray photon.

  • Characteristics:

    • Discrete energies
    • Element-specific
    • Produces K-shell and L-shell x-rays
    • Common in Tungsten characteristic x-rays and fluoroscopy.
    • Threshold energy cutoff: $\(E > E_{binding}\)$​

docs.title: X-ray Production Modes Comparison
docs.text:
Feature Bremsstrahlung Characteristic Spectrum Continuous Discrete Origin Electron deceleration Electron shell transitions Depends on Z Yes Yes Dominant in Linacs Yes Minor Dominant in Diagnostic Tubes Mostly Partial contribution

Big idea: There are only two fundamental mechanisms of x-ray production:

1. Bremsstrahlung Radiation
2. Characteristic Radiation

Clinical linacs produce nearly all therapeutic photons through Bremsstrahlung interactions in a tungsten target.

docs.title: 3. Photon Interactions
docs.text:
Big idea: There are 4 main interactions between photons (X-rays and gamma rays) and matter. These are coherent scattering, photoelectric absorption, Compton scattering, and pair production.

Several factors impact the probability that a photon will undergo an interaction with matter including the energy of the photon and the properties of the atoms making up the material, largely driven by the atomic number (Z) of the absorber.

docs.title: Key dependencies:
docs.text: • Coherent Scattering: ~ Z/E² • Photoelectric Effect: ~ Z³/E³ • Compton Scattering: ~ 1/E (independent of Z) • Pair Production: ~ Z ln(E)

docs.title: Coherent (Rayleigh Scattering)
docs.text: In coherent scatter, also called Rayleigh scatter, the photon interacts with the atom as a whole. The photon changes direction but does not lose significant energy.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• No ionization.
  • No energy transfer to an electron.
  • Photon changes direction.
  • More relevant at low photon energies.
  • Usually minor for radiation therapy dose deposition.

docs.title: Clinical Meaning:
docs.text: • Coherent scatter is usually not a major player in MV therapy dose deposition, but it can contribute to low-energy imaging scatter.

docs.title: Photoelectric Absorption
docs.text: In photoelectric absorption, an incident photon transfers all of its energy to a tightly bound inner-shell electron. The photon is completely absorbed, and the electron is ejected from the atom.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Complete absorption of the photon.
  • Inner-shell electron is ejected.
  • Produces ionization.
  • Vacancy in the electron shell is created.
  • Characteristic x-rays or Auger electrons may be emitted.
  • Strong dependence on atomic number and photon energy.

$$ \tau \propto \frac{Z^3}{E^3} $$ where: - (Z) = atomic number - (E) = photon energy

docs.title: Clinical Meaning
docs.text:
• Dominant interaction for low-energy photons.
  • Responsible for much of the contrast in diagnostic imaging.
  • More likely in high-Z materials such as bone, iodine, barium, and lead.
  • Contributes to increased dose near metallic implants and shielding materials.
  • Relatively uncommon in megavoltage external beam therapy where Compton scatter dominates.

docs.title: Compton Scattering
docs.text: In Compton scatter, an incident photon interacts with a loosely bound outer-shell electron. The photon transfers part of its energy to the electron and continues in a new direction with lower energy.

docs.title: What Happens
docs.text:
• Incident photon \(\gamma\) interacts with an outer-shell \(e^{-1}\)
  • Outer \(e^{-1}\) is ejected.
  • The photon is scattered.
  • The scattered photon has reduced energy.
  • The recoil electron deposits dose locally.

![[ComptonScattering.jpg]]

docs.title: Key Features:
docs.text:
• Photon is not completely absorbed.
  • A scattered photon remains.
  • A recoil electron is produced.
  • Interaction depends mainly on electron density.
  • Dominant for most MV photon therapy beams.

docs.title: Clinical Meaning:
docs.text:
• Compton scatter is central to external beam therapy because it produces many of the secondary electrons responsible for dose deposition in tissue.

    It also contributes to: - patient scatter, - out-of-field dose, - room shielding considerations, - image degradation from scatter, - dose calculation dependence on electron density.

ABR favorite

In the MV range, interaction probability is approximately related to electron density, not strongly to atomic number.

docs.title: Pair Production
docs.text: In pair production, a photon interacts near the nuclear field and converts into an electron-positron pair. This requires a minimum photon energy of: 1.022 MeV, because the rest mass energy of two electrons is required.

docs.title: What Happens
docs.text:
1. High-energy photon passes near the nucleus.
  2. Photon disappears.
  3. Electron and positron are created.
  4. The positron slows down in tissue.
  5. Positron annihilates with an electron.
  6. Two 511 keV photons are produced.

docs.title: Key Features
docs.text:
• Energy threshold is 1.022 MeV.
  • Photon is converted into matter.
  • Produces an electron and positron.
  • Positron annihilation produces two 511 keV photons.
  • Becomes more important as photon energy increases.

docs.title: Clinical Meaning:
docs.text:
• Pair production can occur in high-energy therapy beams, but Compton scatter is usually still the dominant interaction for common clinical MV photon beams.
  • It is also conceptually connected to PET imaging because positron annihilation produces two 511 keV photons.

docs.title: Summary of Photon Interactions
docs.text: Interaction Photon Absorbed? Electron Produced? Scattered Photon? Therapy Relevance Photoelectric Yes Yes No Low-energy imaging and high-Z effects Compton No Yes Yes Dominant in MV therapy Pair production Yes Electron + positron 511 keV photons after annihilation Higher-energy photon beams Coherent Scattering No No Yes Minor for therapy dose

docs.title: Primary Particle Interactions:
docs.text: • Ionization/Excitation with atomic electrons. • Bremsstrahlung: Electron interaction with nucleus creates electron + photon/ • Elastic Scattering - w/atomic electrons: Electron-electron scattering - w/atomic nuclei: Nuclear scattering • Inelastic Scattering - w/atomic electrons: ionization or excitations - w/atomic nuclei: Bremsstrahlung

docs.title: Secondary Atomic Relaxation Processes:
docs.text: • Characteristic X-Ray Production • Auger Electron Production

docs.title: Important Interactions per therapy type:
docs.text:
• Electrons:

    • Direct Ionization: e- place enough energy in orbital e- to ionize (eject) them.
    • Bremsstrahlung: e- is redirected around the nucleus creating a high energy γ and a redirected e-. More predominant for high electron energies.
      • Increases with Z and E.

  • Photons:

    • Compton is dominant for energies 50 keV to 10-20 MeV.
    • Pair production occurs at high energies, beginning at 1.02 MeV, becoming significant at ~10 MeV, and dominating at >20 MeV.
    • Photoelectric absorption is dominant < 50 keV, responsible for image contrast.

  • Protons:

    • Inelastic scattering with atomic electrons \(\rightarrow\) responsible for Bragg Peak
    • Elastic scattering with atomic nucleus \(\rightarrow\) Multiple Coulomb Scattering causes lateral scatter.
    • Range Straggling \(\rightarrow\) Ionization and excitation
    • Nuclear Interactions \(\rightarrow\) Causes distal tail with due to secondary protons, neutrons, gamma rays, and nuclear fragments.

docs.title: Energy Dependence Summary
docs.text: Energy Region Most Important Interaction Practical Meaning Low energy / kV Photoelectric effect Strong image contrast and high-Z dependence MV therapy range Compton scatter Dose deposition linked to electron density Higher MV energies Compton + increasing pair production Pair production becomes more relevant Very low energies Coherent scatter Mostly imaging/scatter relevance

docs.title: High-Yield Dependencies
docs.text: Interaction Approximate Dependence Coherent Scatter \(\sim \frac{Z}{E^2}\) Photoelectric Effect \(\sim \frac{Z^3}{E^3}\) Compton Scatter \(\sim \frac{1}{E}\) and approximately independent of \(Z\) Pair Production \(\sim Z\ln(E)\) Bremsstrahlung Production \(\sim ZE\) Characteristic X-rays Requires \(E_e > E_{binding}\)

docs.title: Attenuation
docs.text:
Photon intensity decreases as the beam passes through matter.

A simple attenuation relationship is:

I = I₀ e^(-μx)

where:

  • I₀ is the initial intensity,
  • I is the transmitted intensity,
  • μ is the linear attenuation coefficient, which is the summation of cross sections?
  • x is the material thickness.

Attenuation includes photons being absorbed or scattered out of the primary beam.

Important distinction

Attenuation describes loss of photons from the beam. Dose deposition describes where energy is transferred to charged particles and deposited in tissue.

docs.title: kV Imaging
docs.text: In kV imaging, photoelectric effect is more important. This gives better contrast between materials with different atomic numbers, such as bone and soft tissue.

docs.title: MV Therapy
docs.text: In MV therapy, Compton scatter dominates. This makes dose deposition depend more strongly on electron density than atomic number.

docs.title: Clinical Example: CT Density and Dose Calculation
docs.text:
Treatment planning systems use CT information to estimate electron density. This matters because Compton interactions dominate in the MV range.

Electron density affects: - attenuation, - scatter, - dose calculation, - heterogeneity corrections, - lung dose, - bone dose, - tissue interface behavior.

docs.title: Does the photon directly deposit the dose?
docs.text: • Usually, not directly. The photon transfers energy to charged particles, mostly electrons, and those charged particles deposit dose.

docs.title: Is photoelectric effect important in MV therapy?
docs.text: • It is usually not the dominant interaction for soft tissue in MV photon therapy. It matters more for kV imaging and high-Z materials.

docs.title: Does pair production happen at 6 MV?
docs.text: • Pair production is possible above 1.022 MeV, but possibility does not mean dominance. Compton scatter is still the main interaction for typical clinical MV photon therapy.

docs.title: Why do we care about electron density?
docs.text: • Because Compton scatter dominates in MV therapy, and Compton interaction probability is closely related to electron density.

docs.title: Concept Questions:
docs.text:
1. Which interaction dominates in MV photon therapy?

  2. Why does photoelectric effect matter more in kV imaging?

  3. What is the energy threshold for pair production?

  4. What interaction is most related to electron density?

  5. Why does a photon beam deposit dose indirectly?

  6. What happens to the positron after pair production?

  7. Why are high-Z materials useful for shielding lower-energy photons?

docs.title: Key Takeaway
docs.text: Photon beams deposit dose mostly by producing secondary charged particles. In the MV therapy range, Compton scatter is the dominant photon interaction, which is why electron density is central to dose calculation and treatment planning.

docs.title: Cavity Theory
docs.text: Big idea: Cavity theory allows us to relate the dose measured inside a detector cavity to the dose that would have existed in the surrounding medium.

docs.title: Learning Goals
docs.text:
By the end of this section you should be able to:

  • Define a cavity detector.
  • Explain charged particle equilibrium (CPE).
  • Describe Bragg-Gray cavity theory.
  • Explain Spencer-Attix cavity theory.
  • Describe Burlin cavity theory.
  • Understand why TG-51 is based on cavity theory.
  • Relate ion chamber measurements to absorbed dose.

docs.title: Why Cavity Theory Exists
docs.text:
When we place an ion chamber into a phantom:

  • The chamber replaces some volume of water.

  • The detector changes the medium.

  • We no longer directly measure dose to water.

  • We measure ionization inside the chamber cavity.

The question becomes:

How do we convert ionization inside the cavity into dose to the surrounding medium?

That is the purpose of cavity theory.

docs.title: What is a Cavity?
docs.text:
A cavity is a small region embedded inside another medium.

Examples include:

Detector Cavity Farmer chamber Air Parallel plate chamber Air Exradin chamber Air Microchamber Air Calorimeter Different material

The surrounding medium may be:

  • Water
  • Plastic
  • Tissue
  • Solid water
  • Other detector materials

docs.title: Charged Particle Equilibrium (CPE)
docs.text:
Charged particle equilibrium exists when:

Energy entering
=
Energy leaving

through charged particles.

In this situation:

Kerma ≈ Dose

docs.title: Physical Meaning
docs.text:
For every electron leaving a volume:

  • another electron enters,

  • energy balance is maintained,

  • absorbed dose becomes stable.

docs.title: When CPE Fails
docs.text:
Examples:

  • Surface dose
  • Buildup region
  • Small fields
  • Tissue interfaces
  • Air cavities

In these situations:

Kerma ≠ Dose

docs.title: Electronic Equilibrium
docs.text:
Electronic equilibrium is closely related to CPE.

It occurs when:

  • secondary electron fluence entering a region

  • equals

  • secondary electron fluence leaving the region.

This assumption is fundamental to most cavity theories.

docs.title: Bragg-Gray Cavity Theory
docs.text: The Bragg-Gray model is the foundation of modern cavity theory.

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
The cavity must be:

  • Small enough to not disturb electron fluence.

  • Traversed by electrons generated in the surrounding medium.

The cavity itself should not create significant electrons.

docs.title: Core Concept
docs.text:
The cavity acts as a passive observer.

Electrons are generated in the surrounding medium and simply pass through the cavity.

docs.title: Bragg-Gray Relationship
docs.text:
Dose to medium is related to dose in the cavity through stopping power ratios.

D_m = D_{gas}\left(\frac{S}{\rho}\right)_{m,gas}

Where:

  • (D_m) = dose to medium

  • (D_{gas}) = dose in cavity gas

  • ((S/\rho)) = mass stopping power ratio

docs.title: Clinical Meaning
docs.text:
Bragg-Gray forms the basis for:

  • Ion chamber dosimetry

  • TG-21

  • TG-51 concepts

  • Reference dosimetry

docs.title: Small Cavity Requirement
docs.text:
A Bragg-Gray cavity must be:

Much smaller than electron range

If the cavity becomes large:

  • electrons stop inside the cavity,

  • assumptions break down,

  • Bragg-Gray is no longer valid.

docs.title: Spencer-Attix Cavity Theory
docs.text: Spencer-Attix improved Bragg-Gray theory.

docs.title: Why It Was Needed
docs.text:
Bragg-Gray assumes:

  • all electrons contribute equally.

Reality:

  • many low-energy electrons stop locally.

These electrons complicate dose calculations.

docs.title: Delta Cutoff
docs.text:
Spencer-Attix introduces:

Δ (delta cutoff)

Electrons below Δ:

  • deposit energy locally.

Electrons above Δ:

  • contribute to stopping power calculations.

docs.title: Clinical Importance
docs.text:
Modern protocols including TG-51 use:

  • Spencer-Attix stopping power ratios

rather than pure Bragg-Gray stopping powers.

docs.title: Burlin Cavity Theory
docs.text:
Burlin theory bridges:

  • Bragg-Gray cavities

  • Large cavity theory

docs.title: Why Burlin Exists
docs.text:
Some detectors are:

  • too large for Bragg-Gray,

  • too small for large cavity assumptions.

Burlin provides an intermediate solution.

docs.title: Concept
docs.text:
Dose comes from:

  1. electrons crossing the cavity

  2. photon interactions occurring within the cavity

with weighting between the two.

docs.title: Large Cavity Theory
docs.text:
For large cavities:

Electron equilibrium no longer dominates.

Instead:

  • photon interactions inside the cavity become important.

Large cavity theory becomes more appropriate.

docs.title: Cavity Theory Family Tree
docs.text: Theory Best For Bragg-Gray Small cavities Spencer-Attix Small cavities with realistic electron transport Burlin Intermediate cavities Large cavity theory Large cavities

docs.title: Ion Chambers and Cavity Theory
docs.text:
The Farmer chamber is essentially:

  • an air cavity

  • inside a wall material

  • embedded in water

Cavity theory provides the framework to convert:

Collected charge
→ Ionization
→ Dose to gas
→ Dose to water

docs.title: TG-51 Connection
docs.text:
The entire TG-51 protocol depends on cavity theory.

Measurement chain:

Raw Charge
↓
Corrected Charge
↓
M
↓
ND,w
↓
kQ
↓
Dose to Water

docs.title: Dose-To-Water Formalism
docs.text:
Modern reference dosimetry uses:

Dose to Water

rather than exposure or air kerma.

TG-51 is fundamentally a dose-to-water protocol built on Spencer-Attix cavity theory.

docs.title: Why must a Bragg-Gray cavity be small?
docs.text: Because it must not disturb electron fluence.

docs.title: Why was Spencer-Attix developed?
docs.text: To account for low-energy electrons that violate Bragg-Gray assumptions.

docs.title: What theory is used in TG-51?
docs.text: Spencer-Attix cavity theory.

docs.title: What happens if the cavity becomes too large?
docs.text: Bragg-Gray assumptions fail and photon interactions inside the cavity become important.

docs.title: Why do we care about stopping power ratios?
docs.text: They relate energy deposition in the gas cavity to energy deposition in the surrounding medium.

docs.title: Comparison Table
docs.text: Feature Bragg-Gray Spencer-Attix Burlin Small cavity required Yes Yes Not strictly Delta cutoff No Yes Yes Used in TG-51 Foundation Yes No Modern reference dosimetry Limited Primary Rare

docs.title: Clinical Applications
docs.text:
Applications include:

  • TG-51 calibration

  • Reference dosimetry

  • Farmer chambers

  • Small-field dosimetry

  • Chamber selection

  • Proton dosimetry

  • Electron dosimetry

docs.title: Bragg-Gray
docs.text:
• Small cavity

  • Electron fluence unchanged

  • Stopping power ratio based

docs.title: Spencer-Attix
docs.text:
• Extension of Bragg-Gray

  • Introduces Δ cutoff

  • Used in TG-51

docs.title: Burlin
docs.text:
• Intermediate cavity

  • Mix of photon and electron contributions

docs.title: Large Cavity
docs.text: • Photon interactions inside cavity become important

docs.title: Check Yourself
docs.text:
1. Why does cavity theory exist?

  2. What assumptions define Bragg-Gray theory?

  3. What is charged particle equilibrium?

  4. Why was Spencer-Attix developed?

  5. What is Δ in Spencer-Attix theory?

  6. Which cavity theory underlies TG-51?

  7. When does Bragg-Gray fail?

  8. What role does stopping power play?

docs.title: Key Takeaway
docs.text: Cavity theory provides the mathematical bridge between ionization measured inside a detector cavity and absorbed dose in the surrounding medium. Modern clinical dosimetry, including TG-51, relies primarily on Spencer-Attix cavity theory, which extends the original Bragg-Gray framework to better describe real electron transport.

docs.title: #TERMA (Total Energy Released per Unit MAss)
docs.text:
Definition: TERMA is the total energy transferred from indirectly ionizing radiation (e.g., photons or neutrons) to all charged secondary particles in a unit mass.

\[ \boxed{\mathrm{TERMA}=K_{\mathrm{total}}+E_{\mathrm{Bremsstrahlung}}} \tag{1} \]

where: - TERMA = Total Energy Released per Unit MAss - \(K_{\mathrm{total}}\) = Total kerma - \(E_{\mathrm{Bremsstrahlung}}\) = Energy lost to radiative processes (bremsstrahlung)

docs.title: #KERMA (Kinetic Energy Released per Unit MAss)
docs.text:
Definition: KERMA is the kinetic energy transferred from indirectly ionizing radiation to charged particles per unit mass.

Equivalently, $$ \boxed{K_{\mathrm{total}}= \mathrm{TERMA} - E_{\mathrm{Bremsstrahlung}} } \tag{2}

$$

KERMA consists of two components:

\[ \boxed{ K_{\mathrm{total}} = K_{\mathrm{col}} + K_{\mathrm{rad}} } \tag{3} \]

where $$ { K_{\mathrm{rad}} = \text{Energy lost to radiative interaction i.e. Bremsstrahlung} } \tag{4} $$ and $$ { K_{\mathrm{col}} = \text{Energy lost through collisional interactions (ionization and excitation, NOT Bremstrahlung)} } \tag{5} $$

docs.title: #Dose
docs.text:
Definition: Dose is the energy actually absorbed locally by the medium:

\[ D=\frac{d\varepsilon}{dm} \tag{6} \]

where

  • \(D\) = absorbed dose
  • \(d\varepsilon\) = mean energy imparted
  • \(dm\) = mass

Under charged particle equilibrium (CPE) $$ \boxed{ D = K_{\mathrm{col}} } \tag{7} $$ because the energy carried into a volume by charged particles equals the energy carried out.

![[Pasted image 20260628205143.png]]

docs.title: Summary
docs.text:
Quantity What it Represents TERMA Total energy transferred from photons to charged particles KERMA Kinetic energy transferred to charged particles Radiative Kerma (\(K_{\mathrm{rad}}\)) Portion of KERMA lost as bremsstrahlung. Collisional Kerma (\(K_{\mathrm{col}}\)) Portion of KERMA deposited through ionization and excitation. Dose (\(D\)) Energy actually absorbed locally in the medium

ABR Pearl

For megavoltage photon beams:

\[ \boxed{ \mathrm{TERMA} > K_{\mathrm{total}} > K_{\mathrm{col}} \approx D \qquad (\text{under CPE}) } \]

High-yield relationship:

TERMA > Total KERMA > Collisional KERMA ≈ Dose (under CPE).---

docs.title: Basic concepts for photon fields:
docs.text:
• Increasing Energy:

    • Decreases skin dose
    • Increases dmax depth
    • Increases dose tail

  • Increasing Field Size:

    • Dose increases in buildup region
    • Dmax is shifted to shallower depths due to secondary electron contributions.
    • Skin sparing is reduced.

  • Increasing SSD:

    • Increases PDD (less affected by ISL, making for a more uniform distribution). Deep seeded tumors benefit from SSDs > 80 cm.
    • Decreases dose rate (length of treatment).

  • Dmax increases with:

    • Smaller field size (less surface weighted scatter).
    • Higher Energy

  • PDD increases with

    • ↑ SSD
    • ↑ Energy
    • ↑ Field Size (Greater lateral scatter pulls the dose more shallow).

docs.title: Factors affecting dose distribution:
docs.text:
• SSD:

    • Further SSDs reduce absolute dose by inverse square law (1/r^2), and move Dmax to surface due to a reduced difference in 1/r^2.

  • Depth

    • PDD in build up, equilibrium, and linear regions.
    • Lower energy components are attenuated more than higher energy, which make the beam harden (increasing size of HVL).

  • Field Size

    • Larger fields have a deeper dmax bc of side-scatter contributions.
    • 4A/P calculates equivalent square.

  • Beam centrality

    • Different beam spectra near heal of anode.

  • Patient tissue electron density:

    • SPR dictates attenuation (Unlike PE which relies on Z3/E3).
    • Compton is independent of Z, but depends on electron density.

  • Obliqueness

    • Build-up cloud makes oblique angles have a more surface level PDD.

  • Wedge or beam attenuators

docs.title: PDD, TAR, TPR, TMR,  BSF Definitions:
docs.text:
• PDD: Percent depth dose
    • For non-isocentric (SSD) setups, we use PDD:
\[ PDD = \frac{D(d)}{D(d_{\max})} \times 100 \]
  • TMR: Tissue Maximum Ratio
    • For isocentric (SAD) setups, we use TMR:
\[ TMR = \frac{D_{\text{tissue}}(d)}{D_{\text{tissue}}(d_{\max})} \]
  • Converting PDD-to-TMR:
\[ TMR = \frac{PDD}{100} \left(\frac{f+d}{f+d_0}\right)^2 \frac{S_p(d_0)}{S_p(d)} \]

  • TAR: Tissue Air Ratio

    • SSD-independent: $$ TAR = \frac{D_{\text{tissue}}(d)}{D_{\text{air}}(d_{\max})} $$

  • BSF: Backscatter Factor

    • (ratio of backscatter dose to given medium vs. air): $$ BSF = TAR(d_{\max}) = \frac{D_{\text{tissue}}(d_{\max})} {D_{\text{air}}(d_{\max})} \times 100 $$
  • Mayneord F-Factor:
    • Can allow for us to switch between PDDs, put the reference depth and reference SSD on top, then switch it for the Dmax term, and square both.
    • PDD\(_2\) = F x PDD\(_1\)
\[F=\left(\frac{f_2+d_m}{f_1+d_m}\right)^2\left(\frac{f_1+d}{f_2+d}\right)^2\]
  • Magnification Factor:
    • Need to use for calculating appropriate FS to use for Sc look up.
\[ r_d = r \frac{f+d}{f} \]

docs.title: PDD Trends:
docs.text:
• As energy increases:
    • \(d_{\max}\) increases (moves deeper)
    • Practical range (\(R_p\)) increases
    • \(R_{90}\), \(R_{80}\), and \(R_{50}\) all increase
    • Surface dose remains high (~80–95%)
  • As SSD increases:
    • PDD curves shift deeper (inverse square effect decreases at depth).
    • \(d_{\max}\) moves slightly deeper.
    • Output decreases (apply ISF).
  • As field size increases:
    • Surface dose increases.
    • \(d_{\max}\) shifts slightly deeper.
    • PDD beyond \(d_{\max}\) increases due to increased lateral scatter
    • If field size \(< R_p\), loss of lateral scatter equilibrium causes rapid dose falloff and reduced output

[!ABR Pearls]

  • Higher energy → deeper penetration.
  • Larger SSD → deeper PDD.
  • Larger field size → more scatter → higher PDD.
  • Too small a field → loss of lateral scatter equilibrium.

docs.title: Equivalent Square Field
docs.text:
The equivalent square field size is used to convert a rectangular field into an equivalent square field for scatter calculations.

\[ a = \frac{4A}{P} \]

where:

  • \(a\) = equivalent square side length
  • \(A\) = field area
  • \(P\) = field perimeter

docs.title: Equivalent Circular Field
docs.text:
The equivalent circular field radius is:

\[ r = \frac{4}{\sqrt{\pi}}\frac{A}{P} \]

where:

  • \(r\) = equivalent circle radius

  • \(A\) = field area

  • \(P\) = field perimeter

docs.title: Match-at-Depth
docs.text:
The gap required to achieve field matching at depth is:

\[ S = \frac{L_1}{2}\left(\frac{z}{SSD}\right) + \frac{L_2}{2}\left(\frac{z}{SSD}\right) \]

where:

  • \(S\) = surface gap

  • \(L_1\) = length of first field

  • \(L_2\) = length of second field

  • \(z\) = depth of match

  • \(SSD\) = source-to-surface distance

docs.title: Clinical Meaning
docs.text:
• Used when matching adjacent photon fields.

  • Creates perfect field matching at a specified depth.

  • Prevents overlap (hot spots) and separation (cold spots).

docs.title: Hinge Angle
docs.text:
For two intersecting beams:

\[ \phi = 90^\circ - \frac{\theta}{2} \]

where:

  • \(\phi\) = hinge angle between central axes

  • \(\theta\) = desired wedge angle

docs.title: Clinical Meaning
docs.text:
• Used in wedge pair calculations.

  • Commonly tested on board examinations.

docs.title: Half-Beam Block Technique
docs.text: Half-beam blocking (HBB) is used to eliminate beam divergence between the cranial and spinal fields.

\theta_{collimator}

\tan^{-1} \left( \frac{L_{spine}} {2 \times SSD_{spine}} \right) $$

where:

  • \(L_{spine}\) = spinal field length

  • \(SSD_{spine}\) = spinal field SSD

\theta_{couch}

\tan^{-1} \left( \frac{L_{brain}} {2 \times SAD_{brain}} \right) $$

where:

  • \(L_{brain}\) = brain field length

  • \(SAD_{brain}\) = brain field SAD

docs.title: Clinical Meaning
docs.text:
• Eliminates field divergence at the craniospinal junction.

  • Produces geometric matching between brain and spine fields.

  • Historically important for conventional CSI techniques.

docs.title: Board Pearls:
docs.text:
Exam Tip

Equivalent Square:

\[ a = \frac{4A}{P} \]

Match-at-Depth:

\[ S = \frac{L_1 z}{2SSD} + \frac{L_2 z}{2SSD} \]

Hinge Angle:

\[ \phi = 90^\circ - \frac{\theta}{2} \]

Craniospinal Junction Matching:

  • Use half-beam blocking to eliminate divergence.

docs.title: Relativity Primer
docs.text:
Define:

\[ \beta = \frac{v}{c} \]

The exact time dilation equation is:

\[ t' = t\sqrt{1-\beta^2} \]

The moving clock falls behind by:

\[ \Delta t = t - t' \]

Substitute:

\[ \Delta t = t - t\sqrt{1-\beta^2} \]

Factor out \(t\):

\[ \Delta t = t\left(1-\sqrt{1-\beta^2}\right) \]

For \(\beta \ll 1\), use the binomial approximation:

\[ \sqrt{1-\beta^2} \approx 1-\frac{\beta^2}{2} \]

Therefore:

\[ \Delta t \approx t\left(1-\left(1-\frac{\beta^2}{2}\right)\right) \] \[ \boxed{ \Delta t \approx \frac{1}{2}\beta^2 t } \]

So the moving clock loses approximately:

\[ \frac{\Delta t}{t} = \frac{1}{2}\beta^2 \]

of its elapsed time relative with the stationary clock.

docs.title: #Activity
docs.text:
Activity (\(A\)) represents the number of particles (\(dN\)) which decay over time (\(dt\)) for a given radioactive source: $$ \ Activity {\,} (A) = -\frac{dN}{dt} = \lambda N \tag{1}

$$ where: - (A) = Activity - (dN) = number of particles to decay. - (dt) = over time

Units of Activity are the following: $$ \begin{aligned} 1\,\mathrm{Bq} &= 1\, \mathrm{disintegration\, per\, second\, }(\mathrm{dps})\[4pt] 1\,\mathrm{Ci} &= 3.7\times10^{10}\,\mathrm{Bq} \[4pt] 1\,\mathrm{mg\,Ra\,eq} &= 8.25\,\mathrm{mCi} \[4pt] &= 3.05\times10^{8}\,\mathrm{Bq} \end{aligned} $$

[!NOTE] The SI unit of activity is the becquerel (Bq). The curie (Ci) is still widely used in nuclear medicine and brachytherapy in the United States. Milligram radium equivalent (mg Ra eq) is a historical brachytherapy unit that has largely been replaced by air-kerma strength (SKS_KSK​) in modern dosimetry (TG-43).

Source activity (\(A\)) decays as an exponential function of time (\(t\)) and the decay constant (\(\lambda\)), using Equation 2:

\[ A(t)=A_0e^{-\lambda t} \tag{2} \]

The derivation of Equation (2) from Equation (1) is shown below: $$ \ \begin{aligned} A &= -\frac{dN}{dt} \ \frac{dN}{dt} &= -\lambda N \ \frac{dN}{N} &= -\lambda\,dt \ \int \frac{1}{N}\,dN &= \int -\lambda\,dt \ \ln N &= -\lambda t + C \ N(t) &= N_0 e^{-\lambda t} \ A &= \lambda N \ A &= \lambda N_0 e^{-\lambda t} \ A &= A_0 e^{-\lambda t} \end{aligned} \tag{3} \ $$

docs.title: Exposure (\(X\)) describes the electric charge (\(dQ\)) per mass in dry air (\(dm\)):
docs.text:
$$ \begin{aligned} X &= \frac{dQ}{dm} = \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{kg}} \end{aligned} \tag{4} $$ where: - (X) = exposure - (dQ) = total charge of one sign produced in air - (dm) = mass of dry air

Units of Exposure (X) are the following: $\(\begin{aligned}1\,\mathrm{C/kg} &= 3.876\times10^{3}\,\mathrm{R} \\[4pt]1\,\mathrm{R} &= 2.58\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{C/kg} \\[4pt]\,\end{aligned}\)$

docs.title: To go from Activity (\(A\)) \(\rightarrow\) Exposure (\(X\)):
docs.text:
\[ \\X = \frac{\Gamma A \, t}{r^2 } \tag{5} \\ $$ And exposure rate $\dot{X}$ can be defined as the following: $$ \dot{X}=\frac{\Gamma A}{r^2} \tag{6} \]

where \(\Gamma\) is the exposure rate constant, with units of \(\frac{\mathrm{R}\cdot\mathrm{cm^2}}{\mathrm{mCi}\cdot\mathrm{hr}}\) or \(\frac{\mathrm{R}\cdot\mathrm{m^2}}{\mathrm{Ci}\cdot\mathrm{hr}}\).

docs.title: To go from Exposure (\(X\)) \(\rightarrow\) Air KERMA (\(K_{\mathrm{air}}\)):
docs.text:
\[ K_{\mathrm{air}} = X\left(\frac{W}{e}\right) \tag{7} \]

where: $$ \begin{aligned} X &= \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{kg}} \[6pt] \frac{W}{e} &= 33.97\,\frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{C}} \[6pt] \end{aligned} $$ Substituting in units: $$ \begin{aligned} K_{\mathrm{air}} &= \left( \frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{kg}} \right) \left( \frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{C}} \right) \[6pt] &K_{\mathrm{air}}= \frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{kg}} \[6pt] &K_{\mathrm{air}}= \mathrm{Gy} \end{aligned} $$

Finally, using Equation (5) and Equation (7), we are able to go from source activity (\(A\)) to air KERMA (\(K_{\mathrm{air}}\)).

\[ \begin{aligned} \dot{K}_{\mathrm{air}} &= \dot{X}\left(\frac{W}{e}\right) \\[6pt] &= \dot{X} \left( 2.58\times10^{-4}\,\frac{\mathrm{C}}{\mathrm{kg}\cdot\mathrm{R}} \right) \left( 33.97\,\frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{C}} \right) \\[6pt] &= \dot{X} \left( 8.76\times10^{-3}\,\frac{\mathrm{J}}{\mathrm{kg}\cdot\mathrm{R}} \right) \\[6pt] &= \dot{X} \left( 8.76\times10^{-3}\,\frac{\mathrm{Gy}}{\mathrm{R}} \right) \\[6pt] &= \dot{X} \left( 0.876\,\frac{\mathrm{cGy}}{\mathrm{R}} \right) \\[6pt] &= 0.876\,\dot{X} \qquad (\mathrm{cGy/hr}) \end{aligned} \tag{8} $$ $$ \dot{K}= 0.876 \dot{X} \tag{9} \]

docs.title: #AirKermaStrength
docs.text:
TG-43 using the convention of Air Kerma Strength (\(S_K\)) to describe the source strength, rather than activity.

$$

S_K = \dot{K} \times {d^2} \tag{10} $$ Where: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Air \, Kerma \, Strength} = S_K &= \mathrm{U} = \mu\mathrm{Gy}\cdot\mathrm{m^2}/\mathrm{hr} = \mathrm{cGy}\cdot\mathrm{cm^2}/\mathrm{hr} \[6pt] \mathrm{Kerma \, Dose \, Rate} = \dot{K}_{\mathrm{air}} &= \frac{\mathrm{cGy}}{\mathrm{hr}} \[6pt] \mathrm{Distance \, from \, source} = d &= \mathrm{cm} \end{aligned} $$

docs.title: To calculate the total reference air kerma  (TRAK):
docs.text: \[ TRAK = \sum S_k \times t  \tag{11} \]

docs.title: To go from  (\(S_k\)) \(\rightarrow\) Dose Rate (\(\dot{D}\)):
docs.text:
$$

\dot{D} = S_k \times \Lambda = \dot{K} \frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho} \tag{12} $$Where: $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{Dose \, Rate \, to \, Water} = \dot{D} &= \frac{\mathrm{cGy}}{\mathrm{hr}} \[6pt]

\mathrm{Air \, Kerma \, Strength} = S_K &= \mathrm{U} = \mu\mathrm{Gy}\cdot\mathrm{m^2}/\mathrm{hr} = \mathrm{cGy}\cdot\mathrm{cm^2}/\mathrm{hr} \[6pt]

\mathrm{Dose \, Rate \, Constant} = \Lambda &= \frac{\mathrm{cGy/hr}}{\mathrm{U}} \[6pt]

\mathrm{Air \, Kerma \, Rate} = \dot{K}_{\mathrm{air}} &= \frac{\mathrm{cGy}}{\mathrm{hr}} \[6pt]

docs.title: \mathrm{Mass \, Energy\text{-}Absorption \, Coefficient}
docs.text: \frac{\mu_{\mathrm{en}}}{\rho} &= \frac{\mathrm{cm^2}}{\mathrm{g}} \end{aligned} $$

docs.title: Overarching connections to remember:
docs.text:
$$ \boxed{ A \;\xrightarrow{\Gamma}\; \dot{X} \;\xrightarrow{W/e}\; \dot{K}{\mathrm{air}} \;\xrightarrow{\times d^2}\; S_K \;\xrightarrow{\Lambda}\; \dot{D}} }

$$

  1. We go from Activity (A) to exposure rate (\(\dot{X}\)), using Equation (6). $$ \dot{X}=\frac{\Gamma A}{r^2} \tag{6} $$
  2. We go from Exposure rate (\(\dot{X}\)) to Kerma Rate (\(\dot{K}\)) in Equation (9). $$ \dot{K}= 0.876 \dot{X} \tag{9} $$
  3. We go from Kerma Rate (\(\dot{K}\)) to Air Kerma Strength (\(S_k\)), using Equation (10). $$

S_K = \dot{K} \times {d^2} \tag{10} $$ 4. We go from Air Kerma Strength (\(S_k\)) to Dose Rate, using Equation (12).

$$

\dot{D} = S_k \times \Lambda = \dot{K} \frac{\mu_{en}}{\rho} \tag{12} $$

docs.title: Unit Conversions

docs.title: Relativity
docs.text: \[ \beta = \frac{v}{c} \] \[ \boxed{ \Delta t \approx \frac{1}{2}\beta^2 t } \]

docs.title: Q1: Time dilation
docs.text:
![[Pasted image 20260616154441.png]]

  • Rocket speed: \(v = 200{,}000 \text{ km/hr} = 5.56 \times 10^4 \text{ m/s}\)
    • 1 m/s = 3.6 km/hr
    • 200,000 km/hr \(\times \frac{1\ \mathrm{m/s}}{3.6\ \mathrm{km/hr}}\) = 55,556 m/s ≈ 5.56 × 10^4 m/s
  • Distance: \(x = 40{,}000 \text{ km} = 4.0 \times 10^7 \text{ m}\)
  • Speed of light: \(c = 3.0 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}\)
    1. Solve for time.
\[ v = \frac{x}{t} \rightarrow t = \frac{x}{v} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^7}{5.56 \times 10^4} \approx 720 \text{ s} \]

    1. Apply Beta factor for time dilation.

    2. For \(v \ll c\):

$$ \Delta t \approx \frac{1}{2}\frac{v^2}{c^2}t = \frac{1}{2}{\beta^2} t

$$

\[ \Delta t = \frac{1}{2} \left(\frac{5.56\times10^4}{3.0\times10^8}\right)^2 (720) \] \[ \Delta t \approx 1.23 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 12.3 \ \mu\text{s} \]

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
The moving rocket clock runs slower, so it is:

✅12.3 μs behind the balloon clock.

docs.title: Q2:  Energy-mass conversion of MV linacs
docs.text:
![[Pasted image 20260615220957.png]]

\[ E = mc^2 \rightarrow 20 MeV = m_e c^2 $$ $$ v' = v\sqrt{1-\beta^2}\]

docs.title: Gamma Analysis:
docs.text: $$ \Gamma = \sqrt {\frac{r^2}{\Delta d^2 } + \frac{\delta^2}{\Delta D^2 } } $$ Where \({r}\) is the distance between evaluated and matching dose, \(\Delta d^2\) is the distance to agreement threshold, \(\delta\) is the difference between the evaluated and reference point at the evaluation point, and \({\Delta D}\) is the dose difference criterion

docs.title: Answer:
docs.text:
\[ \Gamma = \sqrt {\frac{r^2}{\Delta d^2 } + \frac{\delta^2}{\Delta D^2 } }\rightarrow \sqrt {\frac{3^2}{3^2 } + \frac{1^2}{3^2}} = 1.054) \]
  • Since 1.054 > 1.0, then that point would fail this gamma test.

docs.title: Q1: Lead Shielding Thickness
docs.text:
![[Pasted image 20260617211923.png]]Given:

\[ \frac{\mu}{\rho}=0.0439\ \text{cm}^2/\text{g} \] \[ \rho_{\text{Pb}} = 11.35\ \text{g/cm}^3 \]

Convert to the linear attenuation coefficient:

\[ \mu=\left(\frac{\mu}{\rho}\right)\rho =(0.0439)(11.35) =0.498\ \text{cm}^{-1} \]

Using narrow-beam attenuation:

\[ \frac{I}{I_0}=e^{-\mu x} \]

For 10% transmission:

\[ 0.10=e^{-0.498x} \]

Taking the natural logarithm:

\[ x=\frac{-\ln(0.10)}{0.498} \]

$$ x=\frac{2.303}{0.498} =4.62\ \text{cm} $$ Answer:

\[ \boxed{x \approx 4.6\ \text{cm}} \]

docs.title: 8:00: Laura Ceverina - MSK
docs.text:
Overview of ART Workflows: - Offline -> Days - Online -> Minutes - Real-time ART -> Seconds

[!NOTE] RTOG1106 showed 2.8% improvement in toxicity. - Stage III NSCLC - Mid-treatment FDG-PET performed during chemoradiation - Radiation plan adapted to boost persistent metabolically active tumor - First randomized multicenter trial demonstrating the feasibility and safety of biologically adaptive RT escalation.

Key Workflow Pieces: - Imaging - Autosegmentation - Evaluation & Decision Making - Planning

Challenges: - Operational demands - Ethical and equity concerns - Training and workforce development - Infrastructure.

[!QUOTE]

How long have we been contouring? : > 5 min

docs.title: 2:00 Mu-Hai Lin: Direct-to-Unit (Sim free)
docs.text:
What is direct-to-unit (DTU)? - DTU is sim-free to reduce the workload and shrink the amount of time that the patient is going to be at the hospital.

Real-time oncouch planning: - Require pre-planning - CBCT on the couch. - Commissioning of DTU workflow is necessary using an E2E phantom.

docs.title: MRgRT Overview:
docs.text:
Key notes: - NRG GU-015 requires adaptive for bladder (ARCHER)

History: - MRiDIAN came out in 2017 - Unity came out in 2018 - A3I - Aurora 2022 - Elekta Unity Motion Management: 2022

Why is online adaptive necessary? - Accurate targeting - Safe dose escalation - Decrease OAR dose.

Clinical trials: Long list of MRI-guided adaptive trials - MIRAGE trial: Phase 3 RCT between CT vs. - Urinary bowel toxicity with smaller - Pancreas trial: - MARGE trial: Rectum - Smart1: Multi-site UW trial with safe single fraction. - UNITED: H&N

Abdominal Immobilization: - Not necessary and reduces image quality.

Breath-hold Immobilization: - Helps with reducing uncertainty and allows us to reduce margins, and isonormalize to OARs.

MRI-Linacs: - MRidean: 0.35T field is perpendicular and linac inside B-field. - Unity: 1.5T field is perpendicular and linac outside B-field. - Aurora: 0.5T field parallel to linac.

docs.title: Electron-return effect
docs.text:
• Electron-return effect from perpendicular fields, which is less of a problem for parallel B-fields. $$ F_{Lorentz} = q v \times B $$
  • Radius of curvature for MRIdean ~4cm.
  • LINAC is shielded around magnetic field for MRidean

docs.title: Biological-guided ART
docs.text:
Bin Cai, PhD,

Anatomy accounts for: - Size, shape, position, and body difference. - Limitations: Not reflecting tumor/tissue biology/physiology. - Volume change is often the late response.

Biological Information to adapt: - Timing - Scale of functional adaptive - Tumor vs. normal tissue functional adaptation.

What Bio-informed RT can help with? - Patient selection - Dose escalation/de-escalation - Boost volumes.

Clinical example: - Lung NSCLC boosting to improve local control, while keeping lung dose the same. - Hypoxia-directed H&N treatment (dose-deescalation). - If persistent hypoxia 70 Gy, otherwise if not hypoxic, use 30 Gy. - Functional liver avoidance planning (FLAP): - Functional liver volume (FLV) taken from SPECT. - FLAP constraint: V16Gy < 12%. - Functional lung avoidance and response-adaptive escalation (FLARE): - PET for target discretization. - SPECT for functional lung volume. - Contours from PET propogated over to sim CT. - PULSAR: 1. Use high dose to half tumor for months of control. 2. Stop and monitor biological progress 3. Plan the next pulse to patient with biological information.

Pros: Allows for boosting and dropping toxicity. Cons: Uncertain benefit and incomplete outcome data available.

docs.title: Image Registration and Segmentation - Emory, Xiaofeng
AI that enables fast radiotherapy
docs.text: • AI-based image segmentation & AI-based image registration.

docs.title: ART requires autosegmentation
docs.text:
• Precision should be structure specific based on location and dosimetric impact.

  • Autosegmentation models:

    • Patch-based CNN:
      • Traditional neural network.
    • U-Net
      • Most commonly used.
      • Decoder and encoder
      • Balance global context with what local detail requires
    • Attention cascaded models
      • Region--based/cascaded networks localize structures, then refein each organ in a targeted ROI.
    • GAN/synthetic models
      • Shape plausibility and complimentary contrast can help with difficult segmentation.
      • Generates predicted shape, then corrects for it.
      • Challenges by CBCT image quality, so synthetic CT can greatly assist with GAN-based segmentation.

  • Mutliorgan segmentation introduces class imbalance.

    • Small organs dominate clinical risk.
    • Voxel count is not clinically important, so volumetric correction is needed.
    • Region--based/cascaded networks localize structures, then refein each organ in a targeted ROI.

docs.title: Re-irradiation - Rachel Girr (MGH)
docs.text: Patients are living longer. By 2030, over 4 million US survivors will have prior RT.

docs.title: How can ART help address reirradiation?
docs.text:
• Precise dose sculpting
  • Dynamic adaption
  • Safer reirradiation

docs.title: Steps for Reirradiation
docs.text:
1. Prior data
  2. Adaptive contour
  3. Cumulative risk

docs.title: Challenges in re-irradiation
docs.text:
• Deformable Image Registration
  • Dose accumulation is inconsistent methods, with interpolation and replanning adding compounding error.
    • How do we minimize these inconsistencies?
  • Imaging Modality (CBCT vs. MRgRT).
  • Radiobiological modeling (EQD2 and BED missing prior subclinical toxicity).
  • Tolerance doses - QUANTEC, HyTEC, TG-101, disease specific re-RT trials.
  • Lacking clinical trials.

docs.title: Key take-aways:
docs.text:
• Retrieve prior plans with proper registration and dose accumulation.
  • Consider proton-based ART.
  • Adapt when it matters.
  • Build the evidence (ReCOG, E2-RADIe/ReCare).

docs.title: CBCT Imaging:
docs.text:
• HU-based thresholding and deep learning.
  • Hypersight imaging:
    • EFOV (70cm)
    • Larger panels
    • Better artifact reduction.
    • Rapid acquisition
  • Dose calculation:
    • Ethos uses a registration based density mapping for non-Hypersite.

  • Imaging that impacts CBCT registration:

    • Body contour

docs.title: PET-Linacs (ReFlexion):
docs.text:
• Biologically-guided RT (BgRT-based)
  • Reflexion is equipped with a 16-slice fan beam CT is higher accuracy that CBCT.
    • Fan-beam kVCT has higher SNR and fewer motion artifacts.
  • Faster gantry allows for faster imaging.
  • Functional image quality dictates dynamic beam targeting.

docs.title: QA in ART
docs.text:
Whats the risk with ART? - On-table plan modifications introduced. - Workflow complexity is higher. - QA must evolve from static verification. - Errors may occur during each ART step.

What could go wrong? - Imaging - Registration - Re-contouring - Replan - Delivery - Post-delivery review ![[Pasted image 20260618113443.png]]

Differences to consider with adaptive: ![[Pasted image 20260618121200.png]]

docs.title: FMEA with Dennis Stanley
docs.text: Mr. Gasbubble Steps: 1) Small bowel in high dose region 2) Deformable bowel trigger for being near treatment area. 3) Blurred image -> Settle and reimage or breathe hold? 4) Optimization - Choose the small bowel sparing or node coverage. 5) Small bowel moves closer. 6) Treat or don't treat?

docs.title: Round Table notes
docs.text: To lower ETHOS optimization time: - < 5 L1, < 5 L2, and everything else - Reducing the number of objectives and calcing only +/2 cm from PTV extents can speed it up.

docs.title: Lessons learned from Offline Adaptive RT:
Haidy Wisconsin
docs.text:
• Think of the whole patient.

docs.title: Kate Miama, Florida - Workflow Lessons Learned
docs.text:
• Physicist has to be the program lead.
  • Physician staffing is program specific. \(\rightarrow\) Hand-off documentation is the most important.
  • RTTs cannot approve or treat plans per ASTRO guideline.

  • Remote vs. In-Person oART

    • Console-base: Minimize distraction, time awareness, direct communication.
    • Remote execution: Increased schedule flexibility, enables multisite coverage, but risk of attention loss.

  • Plan Robustness vs. Standardization

    • Plan robustness: Is your plan holding? Think of proton planning robustness.
    • Standardization: Templated approaches that are tweeked.

  • It's critical to establish who does what step.

  • Commissioning:

    • End-to-End and looking at calculated vs measured in phantom.
    • Segmentation QA is one of the highest known failure modes.

  • Errors:

    • Imaging
      • FOV - does it fulfill all beam entrances?
      • Image quality and respiratory compliancy
        • Fix: Re-educate patient, faster imaging, or compression belt.
    • Contouring:
      • Long contouring time may allow the patient anatomy to change. Contouring only the organ at risk.
      • Stray voxel can make the optimizer freak out.
        • Fix: Scroll through to look at unintended high dose areas.
    • Delivery:
      • Overmodulation
      • Backward flow compatibility -> Test this.
    • Verification imaging:
      • Focus on the dose limiting organl.
    • Post-delivery review
      • Technical review for first adaptive fraction AND clinical peer review
    • oART Operations Meetings:
      • Occurs once a week.
      • Workflow, scheduling, bugs, and program expansion goals, as well as available improvements.
  • Key takeaways:
    • Standards
    • Training
    • Iterative refinement
    • QA

docs.title: Interactive Planning:
docs.text:
1) H&N needing adaption. What would you do? - Progressive external contour/weight loss change causing a need of offline review. - \(\Delta\) Anatomy change timeline may need to be considered for smarter future planning. - ** Key takeaway**: Dosimetric eval important.

2) Pancreatic SBRT with rectal filling. What would you do? (El Beard et al., 2019) - Adapt same day because its a bowel-filling problem. - IGRT "Snapshot Fallacy" vs. controlling the low-dose spread. - What do you do with air-filling bowels? Does it matter that much for photon electron density?

[!NOTE]

We must "think in 4D" with Adaptive Planning Robustness: - Tweeking anatomy and see if planning technique holds up. - "Physics has to be a good planner." - Templates in Ethos is the one thing that allows us to standardize.

3) Prostate ART with bladder filling while planning. Do you treat? - YES, the bowel is further away now. The reported dosimetrics are now the worst case scenarios.

4) Lung SBRT: 50 Gy in 5 Fx with 5cm respiratory motion - Re-sim with abdominal compression. - Cyberknife with real-time tracking is much superior to an ITV. - What is the latency in Cyberknife? \(\rightarrow\) tracking and training on 4DCT for CK.

docs.title: Jeopardy
docs.text:
What is the effect of motion in blurring targets? - Partial volume effect.

Type of planning that takes into account changes? - Robust planning

Physical uncertainty based on imaging, anatomy, and modeling? - Range uncertainty

Whether adaption occurs before or at treatment unit. - Offline and online

Gain from adaptive benefit vs. risk? - Risk-benefit.

Ethics of access? - Equity

Risk of reducing coverage for too tight of margin? - Undercoverage

Adapting before seeing the - Predictive modeling.

CTV estimation - Van Herk CTV approximation.

Local anatomical deformation. - Deformable registration

Target that needs to be anatomically boosted. - Biological target volume (BTV)
